rationale: Real revenue growth is compounding across the AI wave's primary enablers: Nvidia data-center revenue +35% YoY to $2.37B in Q2 FY22 (2021-08-18, SEC EDGAR); Alphabet Google Cloud growing to $4.63B with operating-loss improvement from -$1.43B to -$591M (Q2 2021, July 27); Microsoft commercial bookings +30% YoY in Q4 FY21 (July 27). Google's LaMDA deployment at I/O (May 2021) shows platform-level AI moving into core products. Revenue is real and growing; the wave has moved from research/buildup to commercial adoption.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro or asset-class instruments without technology-wave exposure. No wave-stage classification is meaningful for these tickers.

IBM's July 2021 roadmap targets one million qubits and beyond (Quantum Computing Report, 2021-07-02), the most ambitious public commitment from any hardware vendor, but this is a research roadmap, not commercial revenue. QTUM is a thin-volume thematic basket; no material revenue or adoption evidence was found for any constituent. The sector is under-owned, pre-commercial, and the biggest news is an aspirational roadmap — consistent with quiet buildup.

Monthly robotics funding ran at multi-billion-dollar levels through Q2-Q3 2021: April 2021 tracked $4.8B (including $2.8B AutoStore/SoftBank), July 2021 tracked $1.14B in 37 rounds plus $1.58B in 9 M&A deals including ABB acquiring ASTI and Zebra acquiring Fetch Robotics for $290M (The Robot Report, 2021-08-01). Institutional capital is deploying consistently into industrial automation and AMR at a pace consistent with compounding adoption.

Rocket Lab, the only pure-play in this ticker universe, just completed its SPAC and debuted on Nasdaq on August 25, 2021 with H1 2021 revenue of only $29.5M and full-year guidance of $50-54M. The stock closed down 9.85% on its first trading day. The commercial space economy is real but nascent — RKLB is pre-scale on revenue, Neutron is in early development, and the sector is actively recruiting institutional investors post-SPAC, which is consistent with a quiet buildup rather than a surge.

Nvidia's Data Center segment posted $2.94B revenue in Q3 FY2022 (Oct 2021), up 55% YoY, with adoption by more than 25,000 companies. Microsoft Azure grew 50% YoY in the same quarter. Real GPU compute and cloud revenue is compounding at high rates, confirming adoption is underway with durable enterprise demand — not merely hype.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG span bonds, T-bills, gold, and broad dividend equities — no single wave applies. Assigned neutral per rules.

IBM unveiled the 127-qubit Eagle processor in November 2021, a genuine hardware milestone, but no commercial quantum computing revenue exists as of this date. QTUM returned 35% in 2021 driven by investor enthusiasm; the wave is technically progressing but economically pre-commercial, consistent with the buildup definition: real progress visible in industry data, not yet in revenue.

US robot orders in Q3 2021 rose 35% YoY, with the majority of new demand coming from non-automotive sectors, indicating broad-based industrial adoption rather than a narrow automotive cycle. The pandemic-driven labor-substitution narrative was translating into real order flow across BOTZ holdings such as Keyence, ABB, and Fanuc.

Rocket Lab completed its SPAC listing in August 2021 at a $4.8B valuation with Space Systems revenue growing 360% YoY but from a small base. The company is acquiring capabilities (ASI, PSC) and announcing new vehicles (Neutron) consistent with an early-stage commercial space economy that is real but not yet scaled. Valuations are speculative and commercial revenue is nascent.

As of 2022-03-31, AI compute revenue was compounding in real terms: Nvidia data-center segment grew 71% YoY to $3.26bn in Q4 FY22 (Feb 16, 2022), Microsoft Azure grew 46% YoY in FY22 Q2 (Jan 25, 2022), and Google Cloud reached $5.54bn in Q4 2021 (Feb 1, 2022). Revenue is real and growing, adoption by hyperscalers is unambiguous. However, the AI wave at this point was still understood primarily as a cloud/infrastructure story — the consumer-facing AI craze had not yet occurred. Valuations were contracting with rising rates, not stretched to enthusiasm-peak levels. This is a surge, not a peak.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are general-markets or macro-hedge instruments with no wave attachment. Stage is neutral by rule.

IBM's Eagle 127-qubit processor (deployed late 2021) was the most recent public hardware milestone as of Q1 2022. Quantum computing remains entirely pre-commercial — no revenue, no quantum advantage demonstrated on useful problems. QTUM holdings are predominantly classical-semiconductor companies with quantum research programs. The wave is quiet and early.

BOTZ was selling off with the broader growth-stock complex in Q1 2022, retreating from 2021 highs. VC funding in robotics continued to grow (+38% YoY), suggesting the underlying demand thesis was intact, but investor enthusiasm had cooled and the ETF price reflected post-crest hangover. The wave is real but equity prices were correcting.

Rocket Lab's Q3 2021 results (Nov 15, 2021) showed rapid but tiny-base revenue growth (Space Systems +360% YoY) and a backlog growing to $237M. The Neutron upper-stage Space Force contract ($24.35M) signals government validation of the medium-lift thesis. The stock had pulled back sharply from its SPAC-listing highs. Commercial space is making real progress visible in operational data but is not yet in stock prices in a mania sense.

Real, large-scale revenue from AI-adjacent compute was compounding as of mid-2022: Nvidia posted record Q1 FY23 Data Center revenue (quarter ended May 1, 2022); Microsoft Cloud surpassed $23 billion in Q3 FY22 with Azure up 46%; Google Cloud grew double-digits in Q1 2022. GPU demand for cloud ML workloads was the demand story. The public-consumer AI moment had not occurred, but enterprise and cloud adoption was already generating measurable, growing revenue. Stock prices had been crushed by rate-driven multiple compression, not by any fundamental revenue reversal — the adoption compound was intact even as prices fell.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro/market-structure instruments, not technology-wave plays. No wave-stage classification is applied.

IBM's May 10, 2022 roadmap announcement (4,000+ qubit target for 2025) and its 127-qubit Eagle processor (live since November 2021) showed credible hardware scaling progress, but commercial applications were universally acknowledged as years away. QTUM held no quantum-revenue-generating companies. The wave was quiet, cheap, and under-owned — real research progress visible to specialists but not yet priced by markets.

BOTZ declined approximately 42% in 2022 (worse than its category), having peaked with the broader growth-stock bubble in late 2021. By mid-2022 the ETF had already absorbed most of that decline. Underlying industry fundamentals held (53% of supply-chain leaders increasing automation investment), but market prices had already crested and were well into the post-peak hangover — names down from highs, time to wait it out.

Rocket Lab's Q1 2022 revenue of $40.7 million and a $143 million contract win demonstrated real, growing commercial activity, but the company was still pre-profitability, the broader launch market was tiny relative to its potential, and RKLB stock was near all-time lows (~$3.79-$4) well below its SPAC listing price. Real technical progress (first booster recovery attempt) was visible in industry data but not reflected in prices — the definition of buildup.

As of 2022-09-30, AI compute infrastructure showed real underlying demand (Azure +40% YoY, NVDA data center +61% YoY) but stock prices were under severe pressure from the broader macro selloff and NVDA's gaming collapse. SemiAnalysis confirmed Nvidia's software moat was intact but competitive threats were building. No consumer AI inflection had occurred. This is genuine progress visible in enterprise metrics but not yet rewarded in prices — the definition of buildup.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro and asset-class instruments with no technology-wave attachment. Neutral by design per the rules.

Yet the stock traded well below its SPAC-era highs with negative EBITDA and a $542M cash runway. Real progress visible in launch cadence and backlog, but not yet reflected in a premium valuation.

As of 2022-12-31, the three AI-adjacent large-cap equities in the portfolio all reported decelerating or declining revenue with cautious forward guidance: Nvidia's total revenue fell 17% YoY and gaming revenue fell 51% YoY (fiscal Q3 FY2023, Nov 16 2022); Microsoft Azure growth slowed from 50% to 35% YoY with further deceleration guided (Q1 FY2023, Oct 25 2022); Alphabet's EPS fell 24% YoY and YouTube ad revenue declined (Q3 2022, Oct 25 2022). AI research was advancing at the lab level — DeepMind's Sparrow, OpenAI's ChatGPT launched November 30 2022 — but no commercial AI-revenue inflection was visible in the financial statements. The wave was in post-2021-peak repricing with earnings and stocks both moving lower.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are general-markets tickers with no wave attachment. Per classification rules, all receive neutral.

IBM's November 9, 2022 Osprey announcement (433 qubits, 3x prior generation) confirmed its roadmap was progressing, but no commercial quantum advantage was demonstrated and fault-tolerant computation remained unsolved. QTUM fell 28.79% in 2022, reflecting the repricing of pre-commercial growth equities. Progress was real and measurable; investor pricing did not reflect it.

BOTZ fell 42.69% in 2022 (Yahoo Finance performance history), making the sector cheap relative to 2021 peaks. Real technical progress was visible in the form of humanoid robot prototypes (Xiaomi CyberOne, Amazon/Agility Digit testing), but commercial deployment was absent and investor ownership was thin. The wave showed genuine industry-data progress — early prototypes, platform-company interest — while stock prices moved in the opposite direction.

Rocket Lab's Q2 2022 revenue grew 392% YoY to $55.5M with a backlog of $531.4M (Payload, Aug 12 2022), and the company secured defense-customer (NRO) launches and a first paying Neutron customer. The Virginia launch pad was being readied for first US-soil launch. Despite rapid revenue compounding, RKLB remained deeply loss-making and its share price had fallen well below its SPAC debut price — the classic hallmark of a buildup stage where industry data advances while investor pricing remains skeptical.

Real models are deployed and generating consumer adoption (ChatGPT, Bing AI, GPT-4 as of March 2023), and Microsoft committed $10B to OpenAI, but Nvidia's data-center GPU revenue was still $3.62B/quarter — essentially flat sequentially — and down -6% QoQ. The revenue compounding has not yet appeared in reported financials; the $100B Alphabet wipeout after a single Bard demo error signals that markets are still price-discovering AI's commercial value, not pricing in proven earnings growth. The wave is clearly in motion but not yet self-funding at scale.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are diversified instruments without a single-wave thesis. The macro backdrop — SVB crisis, Fed at 4.75-5%, gold at $2,000 — is notable but not indicative of a wave cycle stage.

IBM's 433-qubit Osprey processor (November 2022) represents genuine hardware progress, but the sector remains pre-commercial with no vendor having demonstrated quantum advantage on a revenue-generating workload. QTUM fell -28.8% in 2022 and is under-owned relative to AI. Quiet, cheap, and early.

North American industrial robot orders fell 6% in Q1 2023 versus the year-prior period, and non-automotive orders collapsed 42% — a clear post-2021-2022 record-high hangover as manufacturers paused capital spending. BOTZ declined approximately -28% in 2022. The fundamental thesis (labor displacement via automation) is intact, but shipment volumes and equity prices are consolidating after the 2021 surge.

Rocket Lab executed its first US-soil launch in January 2023 and a booster-recovery test in March 2023 — real operational milestones. But Q4 2022 revenue was cut to $46-47M and the company's full-year run rate (~$211M) is modest for its market cap. Neutron remains in early development with no hardware flown. The sector is under-owned with real but incremental commercial progress.

Real, rapidly-compounding revenue was the story at 2023-06-30. Nvidia's Q1 FY2024 Data Center revenue of $4.28B (reported 2023-05-24) and Q2 guidance of $11B demonstrated that GPU demand for AI training had moved from hype to verifiable shipments. Microsoft's Azure grew 27% YoY (Q3 FY2023, 2023-04-25) with AI as an explicit tailwind, and Google launched Bard in March 2023 and integrated PaLM 2 into 25+ products at Google I/O (2023-05-11). Adoption was compounding, revenue was real, and there was still room for further acceleration — the characteristics of surge, not peak.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro/asset-class instruments, not wave-thematic equities. No wave stage is assigned.

IonQ Q1 2023 revenue of $4.3M (Quantum Computing Report, 2023-05-11) and sector-wide layoffs at Rigetti and cash strain at D-Wave in early 2023 confirm the wave is quiet and pre-commercial. Quantinuum's Quantum Volume milestone (June 2023) shows real technical progress, and the UK's £2.5B commitment signals policy-level conviction, but neither translates to commercial revenue at meaningful scale. Classic buildup profile.

Thesis visible in funding flows but not yet in product revenue at scale. Robotics attracted $1.63B in April 2023 alone and humanoid startups like Figure AI and 1X closed early-stage rounds, but none had commercial deployments. BOTZ's underlying industrial automation holdings (Keyence, ABB, Fanuc) are established businesses, not surge-stage adopters. The wave is early and under-owned relative to AI.

Rocket Lab grew revenue to $54.9M in Q1 2023 (reported 2023-05-09), achieved approximately one Electron launch per month, opened a second US launch site in January 2023, and diversified into hypersonic testing with the HASTE debut on June 18, 2023. Progress is real but the company remains pre-profitability with a $494M backlog — indicative of a buildup phase: visible progress, growing demand, not yet in a revenue-surge or mainstream-adoption stage.

Real, measurable revenue growth was compounding: NVDA Q2 FY2024 revenue +101% YoY to $13.51B (2023-08-23); Google Cloud +28% YoY with its second consecutive profitable quarter (2023-07-25); Microsoft Azure +29% with Copilot priced at $30/user/month (2023-07-18). SemiAnalysis documented genuine GPU shortage economics with H100 rental markets clearing above $2/hour. Skeptic Gary Marcus flagged bubble dynamics ($50B in, $3B out), and NVDA was up ~200% YTD — enthusiasm was high — but the fundamental revenue delivery was real and the product pipeline (Copilot, Bard, H100 clusters) had visible monetization paths. Revenue compounding with real product delivery places this in surge rather than peak; the skeptic signal is noted but not yet dominant.

AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro-context assets. The macro backdrop at 2023-09-30 was a 22-year high fed funds rate of 5.25-5.5%, 3-month T-bill yields ~5.3%, gold under pressure from real yield headwinds (spot gold -4.6% in September), and bond prices suppressed by the 'higher for longer' narrative. These tickers reflect macro conditions rather than any wave cycle.

IBM and Google committed $150M to joint quantum research (2023-05-17), targeting 100,000-qubit systems by the end of the decade. IBM's commercial deployment was at 127-433 qubits. Both companies acknowledged fault-tolerant, commercially useful quantum computing as a 2029-2030 target. QTUM held pre-commercial companies with no material quantum-specific revenue. Industry data shows real research progress but no revenue inflection.

Rocket Lab's Q2 2023 revenue was $62M growing 12% YoY (2023-08-08) — real progress but tiny absolute scale relative to its addressable market. The September 19, 2023 Electron launch failure halted launch cadence and pressured the stock. The company was burning cash developing both Neutron and its satellite manufacturing capability. Space economy was generating real but nascent commercial revenue, with the thesis still ahead of financial delivery.

Real revenue is compounding at extraordinary rates: Nvidia Data Center revenue +279% YoY in Q3 FY2024 (2023-11-21); Microsoft Copilot reached GA in November 2023 with $30/user/month pricing; Azure drove 13% overall revenue growth at Microsoft. GPU supply is sold out into Q1 2024 (SemiAnalysis, 2023-07-05), confirming demand exceeds capacity. Valuations are elevated but revenue growth is genuine and accelerating.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are diversified instruments with no single-wave exposure. Classified neutral per the prompt instructions.

No commercial quantum revenue is flowing at end-2023.

BOTZ's core industrial holdings (Fanuc, Keyence, ABB) faced direct volume headwinds. Humanoid investment is growing but not yet in BOTZ's primary portfolio companies and not yet generating industrial-scale revenue.

Rocket Lab completed 10 Electron launches in 2023 with a $582M backlog growing 9% sequentially (Q3 2023, 2023-11-08) and fully booked its 2024 manifest at 22 launches. Revenue growing but only $67.6M/quarter; company remains pre-profitability. Neutron (medium-lift reusable vehicle) is under development but not yet flying. Real commercial progress visible but not yet at scale.

NVDA reported Q4 FY2024 revenue up 265% YoY with Data Center revenue up 409% (2024-02-21), and guidance for $24B in the next quarter — real, compounding revenue. However, sell-side consensus was unanimous, CEO language declared a 'tipping point' moment, and SemiAnalysis (2024-03-13) documented that datacenter power buildout is being modeled at double the 2023 capacity by 2026, implying enormous expectations already embedded. MSFT Copilot adoption was not yet disclosed but the stock was pricing in full commercial success. GOOGL stock dropped 6% on an ad revenue miss despite a cloud beat, indicating the market was repricing any sub-perfection print harshly. MIT Technology Review (2024-01-08) named AI-for-everything a breakthrough technology in a mainstream publication — a hallmark of peak-phase recognition. The combination of real revenue at NVDA, stretched forward multiples, and universal mainstream recognition places the AI wave at peak.

AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are general-markets tickers with no single wave affiliation. Gold's Q1 2024 surge to all-time highs ($2,390/oz) on central bank buying is notable but does not assign IAU to a technology wave. Bond dynamics (Fed on hold, cut expectations repriced) are macro, not wave-specific. Stage is neutral per the instructions for general_markets tickers.

NUKZ launched January 23, 2024 with modest AUM (~$40M), signaling the nuclear renaissance was still in early institutional recognition rather than mainstream allocation. Uranium spot prices reached a 17-year high of $100.25/lb in January 2024 (Crux Investor, 2024-01-31), driven by supply cuts and clean-energy narratives, but the AI-data-center power-purchase narrative was still emerging as of Q1 2024, without signed contracts publicly disclosed. World Nuclear News (circa March 2024) documented discussions of SMR power supply for computing infrastructure but these were talks, not deals. The wave has real fundamental drivers but is priced cautiously — consistent with buildup.

IBM unveiled its highest-performance Heron 156-qubit processor in December 2023 (Network World, 2023-12-04), and QTUM as a fund covers companies at the intersection of quantum hardware and machine learning. As of March 2024, no quantum computer had demonstrated commercial advantage over classical systems. The technology is pre-revenue in its quantum-specific applications, under-owned relative to AI, and advancing through hardware milestones that remain below the radar of mainstream investors. Classic buildup characteristics.

Figure AI raised $675M at a $2.6B valuation (2024-02-29) with backing from Microsoft, Nvidia, and OpenAI, and BMW began a pilot test in January 2024. IEEE Spectrum (2023-12-30) documented the humanoid field entering its first commercial pilot phase in 2024, with multiple companies targeting warehouse logistics. However, no company has yet demonstrated profitable commercial deployment; software reliability, battery limits, and manufacturing scalability remain unsolved. BOTZ's robotics holdings are largely in established industrial automation names (Keyence, Fanuc, ABB) whose valuations are not yet reflecting humanoid upside. The wave is real but early — venture capital and pilots only, not public-market revenue.

Rocket Lab (RKLB) posted record Electron launches in 2023, won a $515M SDA satellite prime contract (disclosed 2024-02-27), and guided to $92-98M in Q1 2024 revenue — real and growing. However, the company is pre-profitability, Neutron (the vehicle needed for revenue scalability) was still in development with an uncertain 2024 target (SpaceNews, 2024-02-28), and the space economy remains dependent on government contracts rather than broad commercial revenue. The wave shows genuine progress and new contract wins but is still in its early commercial phase.

All three AI-wave tickers show compounding, real revenue growth. Nvidia's data center revenue hit $22.6B in a single quarter (+427% YoY) with Q2 guidance of $28B, confirmed May 22, 2024. Azure grew 31% and Microsoft Cloud 23% in Q3 FY2024 (April 25, 2024). Google Cloud grew 28% YoY in Q1 2024 with operating margin tripling. Revenue is large, growing, and real -- not anticipatory. Valuations are stretched but growth execution is demonstrable, not merely narrative.

AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are general-market or cross-asset instruments without a single technology-wave thesis. The macro backdrop at June 30, 2024 was characterized by a Fed on hold at 5.25-5.50%, gold at record highs driven by central bank demand, and a tech-led equity rally compressing relative returns for dividend-growth funds. No wave classification applies.

Uranium prices were elevated ($63-83/lb range in H1 2024 after a multi-year bull run, per Bloomberg June 12-13, 2024 coverage). The AI data center power demand narrative was building publicly as of June 2024. However, no major hyperscaler nuclear PPAs had been signed as of June 30, 2024 -- the commercial breakthrough deals (Three Mile Island-Microsoft, AWS-Talen) were post-cutoff. NUKZ launched January 2024 and uranium equities had re-rated, but execution evidence (PPAs signed, SMR construction started) was absent. Buildup: thesis active, uranium re-priced, but commercial delivery still ahead.

As of mid-2024, IBM's Heron processor achieved utility-scale circuit demonstrations (100-qubit, 100-depth), and 13 vendors updated roadmaps in 2024 signaling heightened competition. However, commercial quantum computing revenue remains de minimis across the industry. QTUM's constituent companies are predominantly pre-revenue in quantum specifically. Research milestones are real but commercial inflection is not. Buildup: visible technical progress, under-owned by generalist investors, revenue still negligible.

Humanoid robotics is attracting large, high-profile funding (Figure AI $675M, February 2024) and major platform transitions (Boston Dynamics electric Atlas commercial product announcement, April 2024), but commercial revenue from humanoid robots does not yet exist. BOTZ's legacy industrial holdings generate real revenue, but the current investment narrative is dominated by humanoid and AI-embodied robotics which remains pre-revenue and early. Quiet but accelerating investment pace fits buildup.

RKLB shows strong revenue growth (+69% YoY, Q1 2024) and a $1B+ backlog, but remains unprofitable and its key growth catalyst (Neutron medium-lift rocket) was delayed to mid-2025 as of May 7, 2024. The company is demonstrating real commercial traction (Electron launches, government contracts) but is pre-profitability and pre-Neutron. The launch services market is growing but RKLB is not yet a proven recurring free cash flow generator. Buildup stage fits: real progress visible, revenue growing, but price discovery and profitability not yet in hand.

Revenue is compounding at triple-digit rates across the major AI infrastructure plays. NVDA Q2 FY2025 data center revenue hit $26.3B (+154% YoY, 2024-08-28). GOOGL Cloud crossed $10B quarterly for the first time with $1B operating profit (2024-07-29). MSFT Azure AI customer base grew 60% YoY with 60,000+ customers (2024-07-30). Blackwell GPUs entered production with Jensen Huang describing demand as 'insane' and orders booked 12 months out. Adoption is real and compounding. However, the MSFT stock drop of ~7% on a 2-point Azure miss indicates stretched expectations, which is a cautionary sign. On balance, real revenue growth dominates and the wave reads surge, not yet peak.

AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are not wave-aligned tickers. The Fed's 50-basis-point cut on September 18, 2024 is macro context, not a wave classification signal. Neutral by definition for general-markets tickers.

The Constellation Energy / Microsoft Three Mile Island restart PPA (September 20, 2024) crystallized the AI-data-center-to-nuclear-power thesis with a real 20-year contract for 835 MW. CEG shares rose ~22% on the announcement. NUKZ ETF gained approximately 70% from its January 2024 launch through late September 2024. The fundamental driver — AI data center electricity demand growing from 19 GW to 35 GW by 2030 — is backed by FERC and Goldman Sachs projections available before 2024-10-01. Real contracts, real operating capacity, real demand driver = surge. Not yet peak because institutional nuclear capex commitments and regulatory restarts are still at the starting gun.

Research milestones are advancing — Google's error-correction paper (September 11, 2024) validated that logical qubits made of 105 physical qubits outperform physical qubits by 2.4x in lifespan, and IBM demonstrated utility-scale 100-qubit computations. However, no pure-play quantum vendor had commercial revenue as of Q3 2024. QTUM's holdings (IonQ, D-Wave, Rigetti) were pre-revenue or minimal-revenue. Progress visible in research data, not yet in stock-price revenue cycles. Classic buildup.

No date-confirmed (≤ 2024-09-30) bullet from this harvest provides a clear directional signal on the robotics wave specifically. BOTZ's holdings data lacked a confirmed pre-date performance figure. Per the grounding rule, insufficient evidence forces neutral.

Rocket Lab (RKLB) reported Q2 2024 record revenue of $106M (+71% YoY, 2024-08-08) with a $1.07B total backlog and 70+ launches in the queue. Space Systems segment revenue is growing faster than launch. Electron is the global leader in small-launch. Commercial and government demand is compounding into a real backlog. Neutron remains in development — its delayed timeline is a risk but not a wave-cycle signal. The wave is in early-surge: real revenue, real customers, real backlog, but still pre-breakeven.

All three AI-wave tickers (NVDA, GOOGL, MSFT) show real, compounding revenue — NVDA Data Center +112% YoY, Google Cloud +35%, Azure +20% — but the combination of unanimous sell-side bullishness, NVDA's ~170% YTD price appreciation by late Q3 2024, capex arms-race ($14.9B MSFT in a single quarter), and the CEO quote 'the age of AI is in full steam' signals that enthusiasm is itself becoming the story. AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG span bonds, T-bills, gold, and dividend equities. These are diversifiers, not wave plays. No single directional signal warrants a non-neutral stage call.

The nuclear wave (as proxied by NUKZ and the fission renaissance narrative) showed real commercial traction in 2024: the Microsoft-Constellation Three Mile Island PPA (Sep 20 2024, 835 MW over 20 years) was the largest nuclear PPA ever signed, Amazon and Google also signed GW-scale commitments, and NUKZ gained ~70% from its January 2024 launch. Utility-scale nuclear is generating real revenue and signed contracts. Fission is in surge; fusion remains pre-commercial but rides the same investor basket. Grounded in the Sep 20 2024 Microsoft-Constellation bullet and the NUKZ performance bullet.

QTUM's 50% 2024 return and AUM crossing $400M reflect rising investor attention, but no constituent derives material commercial revenue from quantum hardware. Price appreciation is outrunning fundamentals; the wave is pre-commercial. Grounded in the Dec 9 2024 Willow bullet and the Dec 13 2024 QTUM AUM/performance bullet.

BOTZ returned only 12.26% in 2024 — significantly below the Nasdaq-100 — indicating market prices are not yet reflecting a robotics adoption surge. The $675M Figure AI raise (Feb 2024) shows smart-money conviction, but the humanoid robot market was only $1.55B globally in 2024 with no BOTZ constituent generating material humanoid revenue. The wave is progressing but is quiet relative to broader tech enthusiasm. Grounded in the BOTZ 2024 underperformance bullet and the humanoid market-size bullet.

Rocket Lab posted 55% YoY revenue growth in Q3 2024 ($104.8M), set a new annual launch record (16 Electron missions), and signed its first Neutron customer. Revenue is real and growing, Neutron pipeline is building, but the company is still loss-making and Neutron is pre-commercial. This is adoption compounding with room to run — a surge stage, not peak. Grounded in the Nov 12 2024 Q3 earnings bullet and the Dec 22 2024 SpaceNews record-launch bullet.

Real revenue at scale: NVDA Q4 FY2025 Data Center $35.6B (+93% YoY, Feb 26, 2025); MSFT Azure +31% YoY with AI run rate $13B (+175% YoY, Jan 29, 2025); GOOGL Cloud +30% YoY despite a capacity-constrained miss. Google Willow (Dec 9, 2024) is a genuine research milestone — below-threshold error correction is a 30-year goal achieved — but experts confirm logical error rates remain orders of magnitude from commercial utility (CNBC, Dec 10, 2024). IonQ Q1 2025 revenue was $7.6M against a $32.3M loss, illustrating pre-commercial scale. Real scientific progress is visible; stock prices have reacted to milestones, but revenue and commercial adoption are negligible. Classic buildup.

Robotics investment rebounded in 2024 after 2022-23 declines (The Robot Report, Nov 5, 2024), with humanoids attracting ~$1B through Aug 2024, but early-stage deal count fell to 2020 levels and commercialization failures persisted. BOTZ returned only ~12% in 2024 vs ~22% category average — real progress visible in industry data and capital flows but not yet reflected strongly in broad stock prices. The thesis is real but not yet adopted at scale.

RKLB FY2024 revenue $436M (+78% YoY) with Q4 at $132M (+121% YoY), announced Feb 27, 2025. A $515M firm-fixed-price defense satellite contract (Jan 2024) established RKLB as a defense prime. Revenue growth is real and compounding across both launch and space systems segments. The wave is small-universe (few liquid public names) but within it, shipments and contract wins show genuine adoption acceleration.

Real revenue is compounding at scale with no sign of slowdown as of June 30, 2025. Nvidia data-center revenue was $39.1B in Q1 FY2026 (+73% YoY, 2025-05-28). MSFT Azure grew 33% with AI contributing 16 percentage points of that growth (2025-04-30). Google Cloud grew 28% (2025-04-29). The Blackwell ramp is described as Nvidia's fastest product generation ever. The H20 China export control (2025-04-15) introduces geopolitical risk but does not interrupt the core adoption trajectory. Sell-side consensus is bullish and valuations are elevated — peak-adjacent signals — but revenue growth is still accelerating, the defining characteristic of surge.

AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG serve portfolio construction roles rather than technology-wave exposure. Gold's +25% H1 2025 rally is driven by macro factors (central bank buying, dollar weakness, safe-haven demand), not a technology wave. Neutral by design for all four tickers.

The nuclear fission renaissance is generating real commercial activity: major hyperscalers (Amazon, Google, Microsoft) have signed nuclear PPAs for AI data-center power (2024 announcements); the DOE re-issued $900M SMR deployment funding in March 2025; and data-center-specific SMR designs (Deep Atomic MK60) and co-location proposals at federal lands (16 DOE sites, April 2025) show the AI-nuclear pairing is moving from narrative to contracts. NUKZ's uranium, utility, and SMR-developer holdings are positioned to capture this cash-flow expansion. Fusion remains pre-commercial but is not the primary revenue driver for this ETF.

Real scientific progress is visible: Google Willow demonstrated below-threshold error correction in December 2024, a key theoretical milestone. IBM's roadmap is executing on schedule and Gartner named IBM the leader in its 2025 report. However, no commercial quantum advantage has been demonstrated as of June 30, 2025, and the IBM Quantum Readiness Index 2025 found enterprises in early planning stages. The wave is technically advancing but has not translated into commercial revenue from quantum computation itself.

Industrial robotics is in contraction: global market -5.8% in 2024 (Interact Analysis, reported February 2025), FANUC robot-division revenue -16.4% for nine months ended December 2024. The Robot Report explicitly described 2024 as 'from surge to sobriety.' BOTZ's largest holdings are industrial-automation companies facing this headwind. Q4 2024 North American orders showed a tentative +8% QoQ recovery, but no confirmed trend reversal is visible as of June 30, 2025.

Rocket Lab Q1 2025 results ($123M revenue, +32% YoY, backlog $2.2B; 2025-05-12) confirm real and growing revenue in the small-launch segment. Five Neutron pre-launch contracts signed in Q1 2025 demonstrate customer commitment to a rocket that has not yet flown. NSSL Phase 3 Lane 1 selection opens $5.6B in potential government business. Revenue and backlog are compounding; the wave has moved past quiet buildup.

All three AI-wave tickers (NVDA, MSFT, GOOGL) reported accelerating, real revenue from AI workloads in Q2-Q4 FY2025: NVDA revenue +56% YoY to $46.7B with Blackwell sold out, Azure +39%, Google Cloud +32%. Hyperscalers collectively committed over $165B in 2025 AI datacenter capex. Revenue growth is compounding and demand exceeds supply — adoption is translating into reported dollars, not just announcements.

AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro/asset-class instruments with no single wave thesis. Macro context as of 2025-09-30: Fed began easing with a 25bp cut (Sept 17), gold at a record $3,858 driven by safe-haven demand, and dividend equities performing steadily. No wave classification is appropriate.

The AI-data-center power-demand narrative is translating into real capital commitments: AWS signed a 17-year, 1.92 GW PPA with Talen Energy; Meta and Constellation announced deals in June 2025; U.S. executive orders targeted quadrupling nuclear capacity. NUKZ appreciated from a 52-week low of $42.10 to a high of $75.03. New AI-demand-linked PPAs are real revenue events for nuclear utilities, not just enthusiasm. Adoption of nuclear-power agreements is compounding, even if new reactor construction is years away.

QTUM crossed $2B AUM on September 2, 2025, and quantum companies raised $3.77B in the first nine months of 2025 — nearly triple the full-year 2024 total. Error-correction research is progressing across multiple vendors. However, commercial quantum advantage for real business problems has not been demonstrated as of Q3 2025; the sector is pre-revenue at scale. Investment is growing rapidly but stocks remain under-owned relative to AI. Classic buildup profile: real research milestones, rising capital, not yet in broad portfolios.

The Unitree R1 at $5,900 (July 2025) and $7B in Chinese robotics investment in the first 9 months of 2025 (+250% YoY) demonstrate that real progress is accumulating in cost compression and capital formation. However, mass commercial deployment of humanoid robots has not yet occurred; Tesla's 5,000-unit target for 2025 is orders of magnitude below any mainstream labor-substitution threshold. The thesis is early and real, but not yet compounding in revenue or stock prices.

RKLB is the primary signal: Q2 2025 revenue of $144M (+36% YoY) shows real commercial progress, and the Synspective 10-launch contract (Sept 29, 2025) adds to a growing backlog. However, the company remains unprofitable, the sector is still small and under-owned, and volume is measured in individual launches rather than mass-market scale. Real progress is visible in industry data — revenue, launches, contracts — but stock prices reflect early rather than mainstream positioning.

Real, compounding revenue growth is undeniable (NVDA $57B quarter, Azure +33%, Google Cloud +34%), but the dominant story by end-2025 is unbounded enthusiasm: Jensen Huang's 'Blackwell off the charts' language, $65B Q4 guidance above consensus, $500B forward revenue visibility claim, and unanimous sell-side excitement. Revenue exists but enthusiasm is now the lead headline — a hallmark of the peak stage.

AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro and broad-market instruments. Gold's 70% rally reflects currency/uncertainty dynamics not a technology wave; bonds reflect the Fed rate cycle; VIG reflects broad equity performance. No single technology-wave thesis applies.

By end-2025 more than 10GW of US nuclear capacity was under hyperscaler PPA agreements (Google/Kairos, AWS/Talen, Microsoft/Constellation), with over $10B committed. These are binding commercial contracts, not plans — real capital is flowing. NUKZ gained 73% in 2025 as the market priced in the AI-datacenter-to-nuclear-power chain. Revenue from existing nuclear utilities is current; SMR commissioning is 2027-2030. Adoption compounding with real (if future-dated) contract volume.

Google's Willow chip (December 2024) achieved a 30-year research target for below-threshold error correction — genuine scientific progress visible in peer-reviewed results. QTUM crossed $2B AUM by September 2025. However, IBM, Google, and all other vendors report zero commercial quantum revenue as of end-2025; the wave is real-progress-visible but not-yet-in-revenue, which is the canonical definition of buildup.

The global robotics market reached $108B in 2025; BOTZ returned approximately 38.87% over the prior year with real industrial-automation revenue flowing through holdings like Keyence, ABB, and Fanuc. Humanoid robot enthusiasm (Tesla Optimus, XPeng IRON) is adding speculative premium, but the bulk of the ETF's weight is in established revenue-generating automation companies.

RKLB posted 48% YoY revenue growth in Q3 2025 to $155M, launched 21 missions with 100% success in 2025, and won an $816M SDA prime contract bringing backlog to $1.85B (+73% YoY). Revenue and launch cadence are compounding with room to grow; Neutron's commercial potential has not yet been priced in given its debut delay to 2026.

All three AI-wave equities (NVDA, MSFT, GOOGL) reported double- and triple-digit YoY revenue growth in their most recent results (NVDA data center +71% FY2026, Azure +33% Q3 FY2025, Google Cloud +48% Q4 2025), with enterprise adoption compounding in real revenue, not just narrative. Peak-caution signals are visible — GOOGL guiding $175-185B CapEx for 2026 and unanimous sell-side enthusiasm — but real revenue and backlog growth still dominate the signal, placing the wave in surge rather than peak at this date.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are diversified market instruments with no single wave attachment. Per task rules, general-markets tickers receive neutral.

Uranium prices reached $85-95/lb in early 2026 driven by AI data-center power demand. Two SMR projects received US DOE federal funding. Canada authorized the first G7-country SMR at Darlington. Meta and Microsoft signed multi-gigawatt nuclear PPAs in Q1 2026. These are real capital commitments and regulatory actions, not just narrative. The wave has moved beyond buildup into adoption-driven surge with concrete offtake contracts.

Microsoft's Majorana 1 (Feb 2025) and Google's Willow chip (Dec 2024) are genuine research milestones demonstrating technical progress, but quantum computing remains pre-commercial — no material revenue from quantum workloads exists at this date. QTUM holds broad-technology companies (IBM, Google) rather than pure quantum plays, dampening direct wave exposure. Industry data shows real progress, but stock prices for pure quantum names are speculative and the wave is still in the quiet buildup stage.

Rocket Lab posted 38% YoY revenue growth, 73% backlog growth to $1.85B, and 21 missions with 100% success in 2025, with Q1 2026 guidance implying ~57% growth at midpoint. Government contracts (SDA $816M, $515M satellite build, $24M Space Force Neutron contract) represent real contracted revenue compounding. The Neutron delay is a setback but the overall launch-services and satellite-manufacturing segment is clearly in adoption-driven growth, not buildup.

Real revenue compounding across all three AI tickers: NVDA data center revenue $39.1B in Q1 FY2026 (+69% YoY), GOOGL Cloud $20B (+63%), MSFT Azure +40%. Hyperscaler capex still expanding ($180-190B Alphabet 2026 guidance). Export controls on H20 chips represent headwinds but underlying demand is evident in guidance. Revenue growth, not just enthusiasm, is the dominant signal.
AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro/asset-class instruments not attached to a technology wave. No wave-stage classification is applicable.

Hyperscaler power purchase agreements converted from announcements to signed contracts: Meta's January 2026 6+GW nuclear deal with Oklo, TerraPower, and Vistra is a real commercial commitment. NUKZ ETF posted approximately 73% annual return on the back of genuine utility and SMR contract flow, not pure speculation.

QTUM surpassed $4B AUM and earned a 5-star Morningstar rating (April 21, 2026) after a 75% trailing-year gain outpacing the S&P 500 by 57 percentage points. An institutional fund trimmed the position citing stretched valuations (April 17, 2026). A potential Quantinuum IPO (April 30, 2026) signals new entrants chasing. Underlying IBM/Google/Microsoft milestones are real but pre-commercial — enthusiasm is now carrying a disproportionate share of the valuation.

Real technology progress is visible — CES 2026 humanoid showcases, Google DeepMind/Boston Dynamics integration, consumer pre-orders beginning — but it has not yet shown up in BOTZ's share price (YTD -2.2% through April 2026). The ETF's largest single holding is NVIDIA, not a pure-play humanoid name, suggesting the market has not yet priced commercial humanoid adoption separately.

RKLB demonstrated real revenue compounding: $602M full-year 2025 revenue (+38% YoY), backlog $1.85B (+73% YoY), 100% launch success rate in 2025, and an $816M Space Development Agency prime contract. Neutron delay to Q4 2026 is a risk but Space Systems revenue is growing rapidly on existing SDA contracts.
